title: Chapter Seven: Lightning Flash
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

"It's simple." The examiner's tone was indifferent, practised. "Perform the fastest sword draw you can. One strike. I will observe and judge."

*Simple indeed.*

Judging skill from a single strike was both narrow and generous. All the variables of real combat — footwork, timing, stamina, the ground beneath your feet and the weapon in your opponent's hand — stripped away to a single movement. This was a screening. Separating the chaff from the grain.

"Shall I begin?"

He stepped back. The examiner nodded.

"I-I'll go first!"

Won-chang. He looked at Yeon-shin apologetically and mouthed: *Too nervous. Sorry.*

Yeon-shin nodded. He did not mind.

Won-chang took his position, gripped his sword, and set his expression. His stance was not exceptional, but Yeon-shin detected the waves of energy rising from his entire body. Well-honed. Refined. Stronger than any wandering swordsman he had seen on the way to Xiangyang.

*When energy is refined, there's no waste. All of it channelled into the blade.*

Won-chang drew. The air split. A white blur, there and gone.

"Wow! That martial artist is different!"

"I couldn't see it."

"I only saw a white flash."

The spectators exclaimed. Gamblers were already placing bets on whether he would pass.

"Not bad," the examiner said.

A bright smile bloomed on Won-chang's face. The examiner took his token.

Passed.

Won-chang returned, whispering. "If the examiner takes your tag, you pass. If he tells you to return it to the desk, you fail."

"How many times have you passed the first test?"

"The second test was always the problem."

"Is there a third?"

"An interview with the Lord of Desolate Sect. Some martial artists come to Xiangyang just for that — to gain insight and enlightenment."

Yeon-shin nodded and stepped forward.

The spectators murmured. Questioning looks. Some smiled as if watching a child's game. The gamblers did not bother placing bets.

"Was he really an applicant?"

"He's a young swordsman with spirit."

"When else would he get to demonstrate before a Desolate Sect master?"

"Even hearing a word of advice would be valuable experience."

Yeon-shin placed his hand on his sword. Looked at the examiner. A nod. An indifferent expression.

"You may begin."

The next moment.

Nothing happened.

The only difference was that Yeon-shin's sword was drawn.

It was as if the world had blinked. One frame, the blade sheathed. The next, drawn. No visible motion between. No blur, no sound, no displacement of air. The spectators stared. The examiner's face went rigid.

"The future of the Desolate Sect is here."

An incredible compliment. Silence, and then — gasps. People turning to their neighbours in disbelief. Gamblers scrambling. Won-chang frozen with his mouth open, as dumbstruck as if he had seen a ghost.

*He saw it. Even I who used the sword couldn't see the strike. But he saw it.*

That was what true eye technique meant. Reading an opponent's attack was also martial arts.

"Give me your tag."

"Yes."

"Hmm. Number thirteen."

The examiner turned the token in his fingers as though memorising it. His eyes held something complicated — wonder, wariness, and something deeper that might have been resignation. He closed his eyes. Nodded. It meant to leave.

Yeon-shin sheathed the sword, cupped his fists in salute, and left the training ground.

Won-chang hurried after him. The glances from the crowd were slightly annoying.

"What is it?"

"Are you... perhaps the son of a famous clan?"

He laughed. It came naturally, without bitterness.

"What difference would that make? I thought once you enter Desolate Sect, only skills, talent, and effort matter."

"You truly intend to pass."

"Isn't that why we came? Not just me. You too, Brother Won."

"That's right. You're correct."

Won-chang closed his mouth. They walked in silence. He was staying at a different inn. At the crossroads he said goodbye and disappeared.

Yeon-shin hoped to see him again on the other side.

---

Three weeks until the second test. Yeon-shin trained in the mountains outside town — the Dynamic Technique, the swift sword, the Fate Conquering Scripture. He honed what he had. Creating new martial arts was a luxury for men who were not about to face combat. During that time, Won-chang never visited.

The day came.

Inside the Desolate Sect, the scale was staggering. Dozens of magnificent halls. Every martial artist who passed exuded a stern aura. A cluster of buildings situated next to spectacular natural scenery, the horizon visible on the far side of the compound.

Among the other examinees, Yeon-shin found a familiar face.

"Young Master Jeong! You've grown taller!"

"Brother Won. You look well."

"I've made some small achievements." Won-chang stroked his upper lip, where a wisp of moustache was attempting to grow. The golden dragon headband remained firmly tied to his forehead.

The second test was a martial arts match — two bouts, judged by the Desolate Sect masters watching from a circle of seats. Winning was not the point. Impressing the judges was.

"Today the standards will be even higher," Won-chang said. "Haven't you heard? They say the White Qilin will appear."

"White Qilin? From the Namgung clan?"

"The very same. Before fifteen, he annihilated a notorious bandit group and killed the Slaughtering Killer, a sword master of the dark path. Around eighteen, he defeated every later-born disciple at the Dragon Phoenix Gathering of the Eight Great Clans." Won-chang leaned close, dropping his voice. "He's an illegitimate son. He can't become clan leader. That's why he's here."

Even Yeon-shin could not hide his surprise. Such strength deserved the title of Qilin — a legendary creature. The reality of the murim, which he had only heard snippets of in Xinye County, felt close now. He had truly entered the world of martial arts.

"If he's already a later-born disciple of the Namgung clan, why come here?"

"Because rising to the rank of group leader in the Desolate Sect would be no less enviable than leading one of the Eight Great Clans. The prestige might even be greater."

They were led to a garden behind the training ground. Thirty examinees waited — some standing with eyes closed in mental training, others staring into empty space. Yeon-shin circulated his Dynamic Technique with subtle movements and watched the matches begin.

One person from each side, called to fight in the centre of a circle of seated masters. Those who won walked north with shoulders high. Those who lost returned south, drooping.

"Isn't it exciting?" Yeon-shin said.

Won-chang blinked. "Huh?"

"Being recognised for your talent. At least twice as good as being dismissed as a weirdo."

Won-chang opened his mouth to respond, but —

"Namyang of Henan Province! Jeong Yeon-shin of Xinye County, come forward!"

Yeon-shin placed his left hand on his sword.

"I hope to see you again. Here, in Desolate Sect."

He walked out of the garden with leisurely steps. Won-chang's shout followed.

"Let's make sure we do!"

---

The first match was trivial.

The opponent was a strange swordsman who had trained alone somewhere remote. He grinned with yellow teeth and declared: "You are the moth fluttering toward despair!"

Yeon-shin struck him in the mouth with his scabbard.

The man dropped.

---
